# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. George Orwell’s 1984 is my favourite book. Which movies should I check out?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** It’s one of only two books I devoured in two days, and I’ve been obsessed with the themes and the oppressive atmosphere of the surveillance state since I read it over a decade ago. Wondering which movies you would recommend for someone who is obsessed with 1984. Yes, I’ve watched A Scanner Darkly and the 1984 movie.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Brazil
  - Monty Python meets 1984. It’s too good. My favorite adaptation. 
2. V for Vendetta (2005)
3. Children of Men
4. Gattaca
5. German film, The Lives of Others 2006
6. Equilibrium


Dark City


Existenze


They Live


Bladerunner


A Clockwork Orange 


The Beast


Minority Report


The Lives of Others
7. You will probably love Equilibium. It's much more violent, but along the same lines. There are also Children of Men and Snowpiercer, the Hunger Games franchise, and The Handmaid's Tale. All are very good. Oh, an oldie, but a goody is Logan's Run.
8. Brazil. My favourite movie, because it's a good movie.
9. Fahrenheit 451 is pretty obvious. Never seen the movie but enjoyed the book.

Its different but I saw the film Moon recently and it gave me some of the same vibes.

## 2. Suggest me some movies with stunning visuals.

**Score:** 38
**Body:** I want some movies which will be having stunning visuals, cinematography which will such a experience that i have never felt before.
Please suggest some movies if you have watched .

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fall (2006)
2. The Cell (2000)
3. I watched Melancholia for this reason. Beautiful movie, depressing subject matter.
4. 2001

Lawrence of Arabia

Dr. Zhivago

Blade Runner and the sequel 2049

Dune part 1 and 2

Tron: Legacy
5. Inception

Cloud Atlas

Elysium

Fantastic beasts and where to find them

The imaginarium of dr Parnassus

Oz the great and powerful

Pan's labyrinth
6. I just finished watching blade runner 2049 like 5 minutes ago, you need to watch it if you haven't already.
7. The fall

Legend

Labyrinth

The fountain

Princess mononoke

Sin citys

Blade runners

Akira

Vampire hunter d bloodlust

Aguirre wrath of god

Good bad ugly, for a few dollars, a few dollars more

Imaginarum of dr parnassus

Brazil

Clockwork orange

2001 space odussey

Big trouble in little china
8. Not sure this counts, but Flow (2024) was visually inspired.
9. A River Runs Through It, True Grit, Legends Of The Fall
10. The Last Emperor (1987)

## 3. Movies like ‘Black Swan’

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I’ve recently watched ‘Black Swan’ and it was so amazing. I really don’t know a lot of movies so I’d like to ask, what others movies in the same vibes can I watch?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 'Perfect Blue' (1997)
2. The Substance
3. Tár
4. Whiplash, Birdman.
5. The Red Shoes (1948)

Ballet (1989)
  - The Red Shoes! Helpman was such a dancer
6. Gone Girl

The Prestige

The Place Beyond the Pines
7. Suspiria (2018)
8. dead ringers (1988)
9. The Wrestler is its sister movie

## 4. I need a movie where a detective is trying to solve a mystery in a small town

**Score:** 23
**Body:** As the title says, I’m looking for a movie about a detective trying to solve a murder in a small forest town. I love films that have the same vibe as Twin Peaks, True Detective, Prisoners, The Wicker Man, Gone Baby Gone, Insomnia, The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo, Se7en etc. Basically I want to be hooked the whole film trying to figure out the mystery behind the events. I’m looking for something to watch tonight—any recommendations?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fargo, but set in the winter in North Dakota/Minnesota so not very forestry

The TV series is very well done as well (5 seasons)

Also not a movie, but the Cardinal series is very good
2. Wind River, It checks every box you asked for and is a gripping
3. Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri

It’s a rural town, not sure if forest, but a great flick
4. Mare of Eastown
  - Agree. Not a movie but it is perfect.
5. Blue Velvet
6. Broadchurch 

Happy Valley
7. In the Heat of the Night
8. Insomnia
9. No Country For Old Men

## 5. 40s movies

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Just saw The Treasure of The Sierra Madre (1948) and was blown away. Any oldie buffs out there got a stack of recs ready to dispense? Can be any genre, but prefer ones with excellent dialogue.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Casablanca.  Double Indemnity.  The Shop Around The Corner.  The Maltese Falcon.  Notorious.  His Girl Friday.  The Third Man.
2. The Third Man
3. If you like Madre, try **The Ox-Bow Incident**
4. Casablanca

The Lost Weekend

The Big Sleep

Henry V

The Philadelphia Story

Brief Encounter

Gaslight (1944) the term 'Gaslighting' originates from this film and the 1938 play it was adapted from called *Gas Light*
5. The Marx Brothers, especially:

Duck Soup (1933)

A Night at the Opera (1935)

A Day at the Races (1937)
6. Not 40's but I've always liked **Giant (1956)**
7. It Happened One Night (1934)

The Maltese Falcon (1941)

 All Through the Night (1942)

The Best Years of Our Lives (1946)

On the Town (1949)

Sunset Boulevard (1950)

Some Like it Hot (1959)
8. 'The Killers' (1946) is excellent.
9. Just taking a minute to think about what an amazing film treasure of Sierra Madre is....... 

I think more and more people will be going back to past catalogues due to the dearth of quality films being made these days.... 

There's something about the old Hollywood magic that few directors and actors can achieve these days.... 

Maybe I'm just too old and too jaded by the endless batman, Spider-Man reboots, and shite like fast and furious franchise flogging crap..... 

Heaven is a place where there's a new Chris Nolan film every Friday a new Hitchcock or Kurosawa on Saturday, and during the rest of the week all the remastered golden oldies are on..... ;)
10. * Laura (1944)
* Here Comes Mr. Jordan (1941)
* Meet John Doe (1941)
* Citizen Kane (1941)
* It's A Wonderful Life (1946)
* and The Great Dictator (1940) on the comedy/satire side

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What is a movie quotation that you frequently use?

**Score:** 2917

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "He chose poorly" - Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
2. What do you mean, "you people"?
3. "Looks like I picked the wrong week to quit smoking / drinking / amphetamines / sniffing glue..."
4. Game over man, game over
5. The Shnozberries taste like shnozberries!
6. Inconceivable
7. Hold onto your butts.
8. My boys wicked smaaat
9. "Get to the chopper"
10. It's a bold bold strategy Cotton.

## 2. Flop of the year is also the best of the year

**Score:** 667

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie will make approximately 200M, which are decent numbers for the current original drama market. The budget was too high to break even, but there's a difference between a flop and not breaking even. It will probably get some Oscars.
  - There will definitely be an Oscars boost. If it wins Best Picture it'll probably get a re-release in theatres too.
2. Isn't the new Tron already on track to be the biggest flop of the year in terms of initial budget and marketing costs vs. revenue so far?
  - Did u see Leto walk into a half packed theatre in Cali? lol
    - Jared Leto is explicitly the exact reason why I won't watch it.
3. Every movie flops these days. Margot robbie and Colin Ferrell were leads in a movie this year and it made $20 million dollars. People don’t go see movies anymore unless it’s part of a franchise. And even those movies aren’t doing that well anymore.
4. Its not the flop of the year, tron, elio, mickey 17 , and snow white will lose more money
5. It’s crazy how much the budget for this movie is. This should have been a $40 million movie not $140.
6. been hearing how good it's doing for weeks, and now it's a flop?
  - it made a lot of money, it just cost a lot to make.

## 3. What film absolutely fascinates you?

**Score:** 540

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie was so good, I always hoped they’d make a  sequel to it.  The closest we ever got was its sister movie [Pandorum](https://youtu.be/PItZ-qr9jG8?si=W2AVAqQ_DZuSLr6t) (2009)
  - How about "Sunshine"
    - Definitely got a few EH vibes in Sunshine when homeboy is revealed
  - I'm seeing Event Horizon in theatres (for the first time!) Tuesday night. It's part of Regal's screams on screen this month. 

I was born the year it came out, so I didn't get to see it in theatres.
2. Event Horizon is a film that should be disposable shlock, and has watermarks of disposable shlock, but the execution, tension, and aesthetics give it more substance and mystique than it reasonably should possess. I think about the film all the time and rewatch it semi-regularly.

I also love how pretty much everyone on the Lewis & Clark chain smokes. Cigarettes on a spaceship seems like a terrible idea but it makes the crew feel very real.
  - It's like somebody saw The Black Hole and Hellraiser and had a really crazy idea.
3. Sam Neil is always good
4. Interstellar.
5. Meet Joe Black (1998). The story is astonishing, also  Antony Hopkins performance is spectacular.
6. The Grey. Marketed as another Neeson action movie but is actually a study on grief and death. It completely blew me away from the poem to the score throughout the movie and especially the final scene. I watch once a year and still love it.

## 4. Examples of characters with little screen time that still became iconic

**Score:** 529
**Body:** Not cameos, exactly.

Things like Walken in Pulp Fiction, Gary Oldman in True Romance, etc.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tom Cruise in 'Tropic Thunder' (2008)
  - First, take a big step back...and literally, FUCK YOUR OWN FACE!
2. Matthew McConaughey in Wolf of Wall Street
3. drew barrymore in scream
4. "The jesus" Big Lebowski
5. Alec Baldwin in Glengarry Glen Ross
6. Anthony Hopkins had only 16 minutes of on screen time in Silence of the Lambs
7. Salma Hayek in From Dusk Till Dawn
8. Michael Caine in Children of Men. Hes such a fun character and is only in the movie for like 10mins but leaves an impression
9. Boba Fett in Empire Strikes Back.

## 5. who are some American actors who can genuinely pull off a British accent?

**Score:** 456

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You know Daniel Davis, the guy that played Niles the butler on The Nanny?


He's from Arkansas.
2. Michael McKean
3. [Bill Hader](https://youtu.be/o6p0W4ZsLXw?si=cwI4AIeKp9mM7DZU)
4. Alan Tudyk in Death at a Funeral
  - Alan Tudyk in A Knights Tale
5. Renee zellweger in Bridget Jones. I actually thought she was British originally
6. Came across a rom-com one day called 'Man Up' starring Simon Pegg and Lake Bell. I thought Lake Bell was American having seen her before but I genuinely had to double check because her english accent sounded so natural.
7. Brad Dourif!
8. Christian Bale’ American accent’s more believable than his Native Brit.
9. Hugh Laurie

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Peter Bogdanovich

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Since he has been the subject of a thread in a while, I thought it might be a good idea to start a discussion about the late filmmaker, critic, storyteller, occasional actor and all-around New Hollywood icon Peter Bogdanovich.

If you're into classic film, you're probably familiar with him, both via his own movies and his writing, audio commentaries, interviews with Old Hollywood people, etc. that express, again and again, his incredible love of classic Hollywood. He was one of the most entertaining directorial **personalities**, a public figure with an infectious love of the art form.

As for his own work as a filmmaker, the received narrative is that he began strong but went off the rails in the mid-seventies and never really recovered. I'm not that familiar with his work from the 80s and onward, but I think it should be noted that he did have a late-career revival as a documentary filmmaker. 

What are your thoughts on Bogdanovich and his place in film history? 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is the kind of hot take that people probably won’t like much, but it’s impossible to separate Peter Bogdanovich the movie director from Peter Bogdanovich the womanizer. The 4 movies he made with Polly Platt, his wife and production designer, are all stone cold classics. What’s Up Doc gets less praise 50 years later than the others because it’s a silly comedy, but I love it just as much as Paper Moon. Targets is so far ahead of its time, but it still works as a movie today. Not many people have a 4 movie run where everything they put out is a 9 or 10 out of 10. He deserves a lot of credit for that. 

And then the movies got worse, and people will look for some explanation for what went wrong as if it isn’t staring you in the face: he split from Polly Platt and hooked up with Cybil Shepherd, and made a couple of Cybil Shepherd vehicles that don’t work at all. I don’t know if Polly Platt was contributing to scripts/direction, I don’t know if Bogdanovich was more focused on making a role for his girlfriend than making a story, I wasn’t in the room! But it’s really not hard to connect those dots. I’m not going to slander him over Dorothy Stratton, again I wasn’t in the room and who knows how he treated her, what he might have done with her sister, etc, but that level of controversy + making several box office flops in a row killed his career as a guy who studios would pay money to direct movies. And maybe that’s for the best, because I liked watching him talk about movies and reading what he wrote about movies a lot more than I liked watching any movies he made after 1975. He’s obviously a smart guy with a great passion for classic film.
  - Yeah, I think you really can't talk about Bogdanovic without bringing up Polly Platt. She's really such an unsung hero in Hollywood, later contributing to directors like Wes Anderson and James L Brooks. It's not a coincidence that Bogdanovic's post-Platt work isn't nearly as memorable.  

Highly recommend the You Must Remember This Podcast's season on Polly Platt.  Goes into great detail on her life, and her relationship with Bogdanovic.  The break-up was fascination.  He wasn't really a womanizer, so much as he was drunk on his own success. He got a lot of positive/critical buzz was living his little movie geek dream, and really didn't appreciate how much Platt had contributed to his success.  His relationship with Cybil Shepard was a part of it. What's wild is Platt wasn't even as angry as you'd think. Hurt, for sure, but she said something like she couldn't really blame him for falling for Shepard, she was upset their creative partnership ended.  I'd argue Platt's post-Bogdanovic work says she was better off, but it's interesting to think about what might have been if they'd continue working together.
    - > he was drunk on his own success

This really comes across with They All Laughed. Start watching that movie and it's just one thing after another of him seeing how much he can get away with. Can I get away with putting my Playboy model girlfriend in a key role? Can I get away with putting the co-writer/producer who can't act at all into the movie? Can I write my own country songs and put them in a movie about New York City detectives? 

And the answer is yes if you made The Last Picture Show and Paper Moon they will let you do all those things but only once.
2. [He answered a few of my questions in an AMA years ago on my old account.](https://www.reddit.com/r/IAmA/comments/38kdsr/i_am_peter_bogdanovich_an_actor_film_historian/crvoh8e/)  Probably one of the top moments I've had on this site and still can't believe I had direct contact with him.

I am also conflicted on who he was as a person/how I enjoy his films.  I enjoy his films.  I respect him as a filmmaker.  Like others have said, I don't know everything about his personal life.  I think he went through a lot.  

I still like him and enjoy his work.  I haven't seen all of it yet.
  - Thank you, I wasn’t aware of that AMA and it was a great read. Actually, I probably would have asked him a similar question to yours about his involvement in The Sopranos.
3. Targets surprised me. The story of how he made it is really interesting. It is als, I think,  one of the very first movies about killing spree, having been influenced by the Texas shootout two years prior.

I liked Last Picture show. Very bleak at times. Supposedly he made a sequel to it that looks very sunday afternoon stuff. Guess he really wanted a feel good epilogue to his semi biographical movie.

A teacher pf mine from middle school used to love Paper Moon. I will have to get to watch it one day.

I am familiar with Mask (probably his most famous movie, also from his post-peak period) but I am not interested in it.
  - Paper Moon is excellent, highly recommended by me.

## 2. How did cinephiles begin to love and appreciate Robert Aldrich and Richard Fleischer?

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I love some works of these two directors and I discovered them by Quentin Tarantino,yeah,I am these type of audience, but I find some auteurists analysis their works in ways I can hardly understand and they treat their works as serious,point-of-view artwork.I know these are a few old hollywood studio directors who made amazing pictures in 50s and stayed strong in 70s. I can understand someone analysis Don Siegel because he is truly important auteur but I don’t know why these two poped out while some directors like Gordon Douglas,Edward Dmytryk,Henry Hathaway,John Sturges didn't. Is there any books can explain these,or Bob and Dick are discussed in film classes in college?I know a Japanese critic highly praise these two directors.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think Robert Aldrich deserves a lot of his praise. He did many great works that are recognised as classics like Kiss Me Deadly. 


I have only known about Richard Fleischer's films because of Kyoshi Kurosawa (Director of 'Cure'). I've watched Boston Strangler because of his praise and really blown away by the film. Looking forward to watch his films more. It's interesting that Kyoshi Kurosawa likes both of these directors and points them out as major influences. Which Japanese critic praises these two?. 
  - はすみ しげひこ
    - [deleted]
      - Okay I'm just reaching here. Kiyoshi Kurosawa attended this critic's lectures. I'm assuming that's how he got to appreciate these filmmakers 
2. I've always held the opinion that if you are able to say that there's artistry in the work of filmmakers like Kinji Fukasaku then any aversion you have to Richard Fliescher is aesthetic at most. He's best appreciated and his legacy should be as a filmmaker or lean, well made and at times ruthlessly efficient crime pictures like Violent Saturday, Armored Car Robbery and Boston Strangler more than his bloated studio films. There's a lot of great films in that filmography and I see no reason why he shouldn't be or hadn't been respected before. I've no clue where larger conversations on him began, I've not really listened to Tarantino talk about films for many years (he's an in small doses guy) but I'd assume it's just another phase of that wave that elevated forgives like Budd Boetticher, Phil Karlson and other studio journeymen who were able to hit autuerial or at least artistic highs through a long career.

As for why he's gotten more foot traffic in recent years than those you've mentioned I'd argue it's in part due to the Inherent underdog narrative of him as a personality and a career filmmaker making great films in the studio system while not being a maverick like so many of his contemporaries. While I can't speak on each filmmaker you've mentioned I'd have to say John Sturges is about the same level as him  in terms of discourse  and respect particularly with his big three classics (Fliescher having more bangers is a help as it keeps people talking and provides more avenues for discussion). As for Gordon Douglas I'm sorry there's not much else to say but just pound for pound his movies are worse. Kiss Tomorrow goodbye is good and Them is fun but a lot of that filmography is pretty weak, when compared to the about a  dozen or so very good to great movies Fliescher had its fairly obvious why ones talked about and ones been forgotten. Douglas wasn't a complete hack like Norman Torog but he was hardly a Richard Fliescher even on his better days.
  - Thanks,your answer is well-analysis of the director.

I have nothing to say about your praise about Fleischer's early work because I haven't seen them.I think 50s noir is like the birth father of modern action movies because 50s noir are self-aware that for audiences crime,violence are what the audiences going to see,while 40s noir don’t.so the crime activities are no more melodramatic,the characters are interesting instead of real-and-complex. Don Siegel's The Lineup is an example though it is not very good.

I am more curious about this [phenomenon.My](http://phenomenon.My) question is more like,why Fleischer is talked more often while Phil Karlson is not mentioned a lot,sorta like that.

I am not strongly agree with your arthouse Kinji Fukasaku example because I am really not a big fan of him.His so many documentary-style yakuza movies,in my opinions,are simply shooting meaningless violence and toxic men without a opinion about it..What makes his movies (I will call them entertaining anti-Ninkyo movies) great in cinema history is Kinji Fukasaku started destorying and corrupting the code,moral and masculinity that established before and maybe replaced them by real-life thugs with beastful behaviors.But he is not raising a question and answering to them. Just like the 70s anti-westerns,many movies includes how evil Wyatt Earp in real life is,but none of these films live up to the level of My Darling Clemtine. 

I am not wise enough to analysis movies well and my Richard Fleischer movies are The Narrow Margin,The Last Run,The New Centurions and great Elmore Leonard scripted Mr.Majestyk.All with strong Tarantino fingerprints.
    - Can't agree less on your opinions on Fukasaku, anti-westerns  or the lineup but I'll digress and not make this a conversation about those things. To each their own.

Why is Fliescher more discussed than Phil Karlson? Well in my film circles I'd say he's definitely not. They stand about equal as referenced and discussed by filmmakers, though I'm also a die hard Joe Don Baker fan so Karlson to me is what Siegel is to Clint super fans (also guilty of that).

 For the larger world of film discussion I'd say it really just comes down to two things Firstly Richard Fliescher made a lot of movies with a lot of still popular stars like Kirk Douglas, Tony Curtis, Mia Farrow, Henry Fonda, Orson Welles, George C Scott, Stacy Keach... Actors who remain popular to this day and have people searching for other great films with them. You just saw Hardcore or Exorcist 3 and are now looking for another great C Scott movie, "hey this film called the last Centurions has good reviews, I'll check that out". That is definitely part of the perpetual foot traffic towards movies of his.

Secondly I'd argue Fliescher is just a more varied filmmaker than Karlson. Karlson was often a gun for hire who only became invested when a gritty film about institutional corruption and violence appeared. He had a niche he thrived in and much of the rest of his filmography was him on autopilot in things like Kid Galahad. Those great movies are incredible but they're a particular set that will allow Cinema fans who don't often search them out to miss them.  Fliescher meanwhile definitely had his for hire gigs (everything after Mandingo for example) but he also seemed to commit himself more to a variety of films. He thrived in lean crime films and dramas but would equally throw himself into historical epics like Vikings or Barrabas, big studio pictures like 2000 Leagues, Science Fiction like Fantastic voyage and Soylant Green. This led to a more varied filmography and a higher chance of people seeing at least a handful of his films each. 

This isn't a value judgment on either filmmaker, on their best days I think both were absolutely fantastic filmmakers. I'm just saying it makes sense to me why Fliescher is more discussed when looking at those two filmographies. 

I'd also add Fliescher has a unique position of being a bit of a contradiction . An inarguable studio man who was by all accounts happy to truck along as a career  filmmaker making star pictures without ever making waves like say a Budd Beotticher did. Yet someone whose personal tastes were almost in direct contradiction to that as he made his bones in RKO gritty crime pictures and never lost that part of him. It's telling that his one-for-me film after managing the disaster of Dr Dolittle was the Boston Strangler. It seems bizarre that the man who made Tora Tora Tora or 2000 Leagues made a film as unpleasant and raw as Mandingo but you could see the man who made Armored Car Robbery making it. They're almost two careers occurring at the same time and it's a good subject of conversation. Also the fact that he was such a pleasant yet often disrespected figure in his career makes him a nice underdog.
      - I agree with all you said about Fleischer and Karlson and sadly I don't know anybody strongly champion Karlson.I guess the problem is I don't know how big Dick's studio movies are.

John Flynn's The Outfit and Phil Karlson's Framed are MASTERPIECE for me without equal. I have been in an Ethan Edwards mood in chasing 70s movies with same level quality and direction. They happened to be both Joe Don Baker's movies.And these two gave me the 50s-noir-birth father-of-action cinema idea
        - I see a lot of love for Karlson with people I know who like film and the critics I read but I can see if you aren't already in a millue not seeing much about him.  I'll personally champion him and I do consider it a tragedy that he and Baker made only two films together. they were tailor made for one another. I wish Karlson had been just a bit younger and had a few more as they had the potential to be the grimier, meaner counterparts to Don and Clint. Still they made Framed which is an inarguable achievement.

## 3. Short silent film screening event

**Score:** 2
**Body:** Hi, I'm organising an event which will screen around 4/5 short silent films. All of the films are in the public domain in the UK (late-1920s). 

Where do I download the films from? All of the films can be found on YouTube. The event will either take place in a cinema or another arts venue. Is there somewhere online where I can access copies of the films which are in the right format/quality to hand across to the cinemas?

This is my first time organising an event like this and so I want to make sure I'm going about things correctly. Thanks for your help in advance!

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 1. You can't assume the films are public domain simply because of age. This is quite a complex area. Overlaid scores are almost certainly not public domain. Restoration work may have taken place at great expense and *that* version won't be public domain. But if you had a 16mm cinema club print, that *would* be. Possibly. Not simple at all.

2. Proper cinemas use DCPs now and nothing else. A very rare one will still be able to screen 16/35mm. Streaming YouTube quality will look absolutely atrocious on a cinema sized screen. It doesn't look very good on a 65" TV.

The BFI and Independent Cinema Office will have film club contacts still potentially (they kind of died off a bit with digital). They're very approachable and would be able to help sourcing. Your cinema will be able to tell you what they can screen.
2. Talk to the theater that you're working with and ask what format they want.

They will definitely have the ability to play DCP or a DVD/bluray.  If it's an independent theater they might have a 35mm projector but you probably can't get prints of the films you're screening.
3. Look for sites devoted to specific silent artists to see if they include publicly-available copies: Chaplin, Griffith, Keaton etc.  So many sites devoted to film and specific directs/artists that you are bound to find something good.  For instance, [https://worldofbusterkeaton.com/](https://worldofbusterkeaton.com/)

Be careful: here in the US it made a big difference in terms of cost and liability if I showed a film on campus from a hard copy or from a streaming service. It mattered also whether admission was free or not; in cases where a paid admission made the use of material difficult, a simple "donation" was requested in lieu of a paid ticket.  good luck!

## 4. The Shining - Analysis

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Rewatching it two years after my first viewing, I spent the entire film on edge. Knowing how the story unfolds makes you pay much closer attention to the characters’ behavior and every small detail. Whoever says this movie isn’t scary clearly has no idea what they’re talking about.

I’m fascinated by all the hidden details and the themes explored—such as abuse, the consequences of addiction, and the critique of the Native American massacre carried out by the United States. This is made clear when it’s mentioned that the hotel was built on top of an Indigenous burial ground, and through the Native-inspired art that decorates it. The elevator’s flood of blood, for instance, can symbolize all the Indigenous people who were killed—while also serving as a subtle parallel to Nazi Germany, with small details that may or may not be just the audience’s paranoia.

One detail I really liked is how Jack represents the evil of the hotel itself. There are two clear clues that suggest a past version of Jack existed. The first one is obvious—the photo at the end of the film from the July 4th ball (ironically, of all days). The second is subtler—the twins.
At the beginning of the movie, during the interview scene, the girls are mentioned as having been murdered by their father, Charles Grady (who later appears as Delbert Grady—another hint at reincarnation). In that story, the daughters are said to be 8 and 10 years old, but in the film, they’re shown as identical twins. Another sign that there were two Gradys—just like Jack—each a caretaker of the hotel.

Another theory I’d like to explore is the idea of Jack having abused Danny during his childhood, which is subtly suggested in three moments. First, the bar scene, where Jack orders a bourbon. Then, the scene where Danny is surrounded by a teddy bear in their house, connecting to the infamous moment where a man in a bear/dog costume is performing oral sex on one of the hotel’s previous owners (which could symbolize the Overlook’s corrupt past).
In the bar scene, Jack orders a bourbon, but the bartender serves him Jack Daniels. Knowing Kubrick, I don’t think it’s far-fetched to see that as a deliberate connection between Jack and Danny—the two names that make up the drink’s brand. But why is this moment so important beyond that wordplay? Because throughout the movie, we see clear signs of Jack’s alcoholism, which feeds his downfall and madness. Just before meeting Lloyd, he even says he’d “give his soul for a drink,” revealing not only his addiction but also hinting at the possibility of other forms of abuse that Danny might have suffered at his hands.

Now, the second reference—the man in the bear/dog suit and the hotel owner—can be interpreted as a metaphor for child sexual abuse. Apart from the earlier scene where Jack admits he once dislocated Danny’s shoulder, this disturbing image appears precisely when Wendy begins to uncover the hotel’s darkest horrors—symbolically unveiling her husband’s past abuse of their son.

I could go on about other theories that fascinate me, because I absolutely love this film—it’s packed with symbolism and hidden meaning worthy of a genius. No matter how much of a bastard Kubrick was, or how terribly Shelley Duvall suffered during filming, this movie remains a masterpiece.

P.S. – Extra details I liked but didn’t get to discuss:

Halloran’s death happens right beneath the only lit chandelier (“the shining”).

The elevator behind Jack’s writing desk—the floor indicators look like two eyes staring at him (a theory reinforced by the film’s poster).

The shots where Danny appears to be “targeted” by the kitchen knives hanging behind him.

Tony living inside Danny after the abuse—as a coping mechanism to protect his innocence. After visiting Room 237, Tony fully takes over, shielding Danny from what he has seen and what’s still to come.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have you seen the other Kubricks? Clockwork Orange, Barry Lyndon, Eyes Wide Shut, and The Shining, which is terrifying and no one even can agree on what’s happening. 

Francis Ford Coppola’s The Conversation and Apocalypse Now are both great, dense texts. 

Speaking of ambiguity, David Lynch is great, you could start with Mullholland Drive. David Cronenberg, if you don’t mind horror, something like Videodrome. 

If you’re willing to go into foreign film, Tarkovsky is one of the big ones in more philosophical, contemplative cinema. 

Terrence Malick—the new world or the thin red line—if you want something poetic. 

You are probably familiar with more recent films, but Midsommar and Nope are both rich, dense texts.
2. - The Thin Red Line
- Annihilation
- Stalker
- Solaris

Malick and Tarkovsky are littered with such meditative films

Maybe Ikuru and Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind for films with depth but more traditional narratives
3. Persona (1966) by Ingmar Bergman is the greatest movie ever made and fits the description you gave.
